title: Copy and assignment semantics

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Copy and assignment semantics; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: C++ Core Guidelines; living guidelines accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Copy and assignment semantics
sources.claim: Copy construction and assignment create or replace independent object state according to value semantics, while compiler-generated special members perform memberwise operations.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Copy construction and assignment create or replace independent object state according to value semantics, while compiler-generated special members perform memberwise operations.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A class containing std::vector safely uses generated copy operations because the vector owns and copies its elements. Your goal is to predict and test copying and assignment for a value-like class. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Value, reference and const-reference parameters; pf data records; Constructors and initialization. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Copy construction and assignment create or replace independent object state according to value semantics, while compiler-generated special members perform memberwise operations. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Raw ownership, self-assignment, base members, exception safety, and user-declared destructor or move operations affect generated special-member behavior. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Compiler-generated copying always performs a deep copy of every resource a class can reach.

includes: Reason about independent values, memberwise copying and when compiler-generated operations suffice.
Predict and test copying and assignment for a value-like class.
excludes: Raw ownership, self-assignment, base members, exception safety, and user-declared destructor or move operations affect generated special-member behavior.

outcomes: Predict and test copying and assignment for a value-like class.

examples.title: Copy and assignment semantics: defined case
examples.situation: A class containing std::vector safely uses generated copy operations because the vector owns and copies its elements.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A class containing std::vector safely uses generated copy operations because the vector owns and copies its elements.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Generated copying is memberwise; use RAII value members or define deliberate copy semantics when memberwise behavior does not represent an independent value.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Generated copying is memberwise; use RAII value members or define deliberate copy semantics when memberwise behavior does not represent an independent value.

examples.title: Copy and assignment semantics: boundary case
examples.situation: A raw owning pointer would be copied shallowly by a generated copy operation, risking double deletion, so ownership is redesigned with a value or smart pointer.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A raw owning pointer would be copied shallowly by a generated copy operation, risking double deletion, so ownership is redesigned with a value or smart pointer.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Raw ownership, self-assignment, base members, exception safety, and user-declared destructor or move operations affect generated special-member behavior.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Compiler-generated copying always performs a deep copy of every resource a class can reach.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Predict and test copying and assignment for a value-like class.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Compiler-generated copying always performs a deep copy of every resource a class can reach.
misconceptions.correction: Generated copying is memberwise; use RAII value members or define deliberate copy semantics when memberwise behavior does not represent an independent value.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Compiler-generated copying always performs a deep copy of every resource a class can reach.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Copy and assignment semantics: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Raw ownership, self-assignment, base members, exception safety, and user-declared destructor or move operations affect generated special-member behavior.

recap.summary: You should now be able to predict and test copying and assignment for a value-like class. Keep this boundary in view: Raw ownership, self-assignment, base members, exception safety, and user-declared destructor or move operations affect generated special-member behavior.
